focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

By dawn the substrate had gone quiet.

Gavin noticed it the way you notice a headache ending — not the sudden absence of pressure but the slow realization that it had been there and now it wasn't. For hours the battle's output had saturated everything: hundreds of spells fired simultaneously, each one flexing the agreements that made it possible, the accumulated noise of it drowning out the fine structures he needed to perceive the way a brass band drowns out a whisper. He'd sat with the manuscript open on his knees and tried to catch the pattern before it flattened back into notation, but every time his perception tried to settle, another volley of combat magic dragged the substrate sideways and the patterns dissolved.

He didn't know how long the battle had lasted. Time had a different quality in the sub-collection even on normal nights — the old stone didn't carry sound from the castle above, and the self-lit walls held the same faint glow whether it was noon or midnight. But the substrate carried everything, and through it Gavin had felt the fighting as a continuous distortion, the magical equivalent of trying to read during an earthquake. He'd tried to work anyway. He'd sat on the limestone floor with his back against the shelf where he'd first found the manuscript seven months ago, and he'd held the pages open, and he'd stared at notation that blurred and re-formed every time a cluster of killing curses hit the same stretch of corridor two hundred feet above his head. He'd accomplished nothing.

And then it stopped. There was a trailing-off, a scattering of final percussive jolts — single spells, then silence, then another, then silence that held — and then a quiet that spread outward through the substrate until the last resonance died out. The agree...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...owed by the wet cough of somebody trying not to choke. Once the old stone overhead rang hard enough to sting his teeth. It was still the last night of April, the castle filling and bracing before the battle, but even that much pressure was enough to rough up the substrate until the building hummed with it.

He took the manuscript, the quartz weights, and his notes into the innermost room and set them on the stone table there. It put more wall between him and the blocked corridor, though not enough to shut the noise out. The floor still answered distant impacts. Dust still shook loose from the ceiling joints in fine pale threads.

He sat in the dry limestone cold of the innermost room, the manuscript open before him on the stone table, the quartz weights ticking whenever the floor answered the noise above. The wards overhead were thin and thinning. The thing beyond them continued in its way, mindless and incompatible with everything that existed below the wards in the way too much load was incompatible with a beam already splitting along the grain: through the dull fact of pressure. His usual route out was gone. Smoke and blood smell had reached even here. The notation jittered on the page.

Gavin Tench, fifteen years old, mediocre at everything except the one thing that mattered and not good enough at that, sat at the stone table with the blocked corridor somewhere beyond two turns of old limestone and tried to keep the Builders' notation steady in his head while the world above him braced for a fight that had not started yet, over a problem he could solve in seconds and wouldn't, because the wards were failing, and the wards were all that mattered, and the wards did not care about the war.

He brushed dust from the margin and turned a page. The notation moved. He read.
